Nutrient-Growth Report

Single-cell simulation exercising the extracted metabolic_kinetics step with Michaelis-Menten glucose uptake. Target: parameterize growth under varying nutrient conditions against the Caglar et al. 2017 (srep45303) multi-condition dataset committed under data/caglar2017/.

Growth trajectory

Glucose uptake kinetics

With Michaelis-Menten enabled in metabolic_kinetics.py, the glucose import bound is computed every step from boundary.external.GLC instead of being fixed by a media lookup. Parameters: v_max = 20.0 mmol/gDCW/h, K_m = 0.01 mM.

Exchange fluxes

Counts of molecules moving across the cell boundary per simulation step (negative = cell imports, positive = cell secretes). Y-axis is symlog so zero crossings and values spanning multiple decades are both visible. Red dashed line marks when [GLC]ₑₓₜ first drops below 0.01 mM.

What picks up the slack when glucose cuts off?

Mean flux in snapshots with plenty of glucose (>1 mM) vs. snapshots after depletion (<0.01 mM). Biggest absolute shifts first.

External nutrient concentrations

Every boundary species metabolism.py exchanges with is dynamically updated by environment_update.py. This panel shows which nutrients drain first. Ammonium typically runs out alongside glucose in the default 10 fL environment; phosphate, sulfate, and trace ions decay more slowly.

Carbon accounting

Emitted by the carbon_budget_listener each step (values in mmol C). Imports are weighted by per-molecule carbon counts from v2ecoli/library/carbon_counts.py. Biomass C is estimated from dry-mass delta at 48% C/g DCW.

Reading the plot: in a carbon-balanced cell, the green line (C in) should exceed the sum of red (C out) and blue (C into biomass). When the red-filled region on the right panel grows after glucose depletion, the cell is secreting more carbon than it imports — i.e. draining internal pools to keep the homeostatic objective happy. That pink area is the quantitative signature of the "pool-drain" failure mode.

What's actually growing?

Dry-mass composition over time. If carbon isn't coming in from the environment, the question is which biomass pools are still gaining mass — and the answer is all of them: protein, RNA, DNA, and small molecules all continue to accumulate past glucose depletion. That growth has to come from somewhere; see the mass balance below.

Mass-balance check

A closed cell must satisfy Σ imports − Σ secretions = Σ dry-mass-gained (in fg, ignoring water which doesn't contribute to dry mass). Any persistent negative deficit on the right panel means the LP is synthesizing biomass faster than it's importing mass — a direct quantitative symptom of the homeostatic-objective pool-drain pathology. This is the metric any further mechanistic fix should drive toward zero.

How is biomass being manufactured?

wholecell's modular_fba implements a homeostatic objective using per-target quadratic slack pseudofluxes named quadFractionFromUnity. These slacks represent "the amount by which a metabolite's production missed its target"; they carry a quadratic penalty in the objective but are bounded [-∞, +∞] on flux (see modular_fba.py line 596). That means the LP can always "close the gap" on any homeostatic target by running these slacks — even when no carbon is coming in from the boundary.

The FBA also has a built-in mass-accounting pseudometabolite (_massExchangeID) that sums net mass in/out through exchange reactions only, exposed via setMaxMassAccumulated(bound). Experimentally setting this bound to ~0 (exchange-mass accumulation forbidden) has no effect on dry-mass growth — confirming the mass is entering via the quadratic slacks, not the exchanges.

Dark-matter pool (diagnostic)

A "biomass dark matter" accountant. Each step:

dark_matter += (Σ bulk mass change) − (Σ exchange mass change)

where bulk-mass uses molecular weights for every molecule in sim_data (100% coverage, 12,809 entries). Invariant: mass can't be created, so dark_matter ≥ 0 should stay near zero. Sustained upward drift = LP manufacturing mass without a boundary source.

Phase 1 just measures and reports. Phase 2 adds a dark-matter flux to the LP with one-sided bounds and an objective-minimised withdrawal, so the LP tries to stay at pool=0 and is infeasible only when biomass targets genuinely can't be met (which is fine — cell stays at whatever growth the carbon actually allows).

Phase 2: enforcement at the metabolism step

metabolism.py now wraps the FBA solve in a mass-balance check. Each step:

Δ cell_mass = Σ Δcount × MW   (from LP)
Δ boundary_mass = imports − secretions  (from exchange dict)
excess = Δ cell_mass − Δ boundary_mass
if excess > pool_fg: scale = (boundary + pool) / cell_mass

All LP-proposed count deltas are multiplied by scale before being written to bulk. This preserves stoichiometric ratios (if S·v=0 held for the LP solution, it holds for v × scale) while enforcing net cell mass change ≤ boundary imports + available pool. When pool is empty and no carbon is imported, scale → 0 and growth freezes — exactly the stationary-phase behavior we want.

Numbers with 10 fL env, 600s run:

Time[GLC] (mM)Pool (fg)Scale
30s9.20.00.992
120s2.60.00.992
180s0.00.00.524
300s0.00.00.000

The scale drops smoothly from 0.992 (full glucose; the residual 0.008 reflects precision in the boundary-mass vs cell-mass computation) to 0.524 (glucose just gone, internal pools still usable) to 0.0 (cell frozen — no carbon available, growth stops). This is the stationary-phase transition driven entirely by mass conservation.

The orange dashed curve is what wholecell's LP thinks has accumulated through exchanges. The blue curve is actual dry-mass gain. They track each other pre-shutoff (cell is closed) and diverge post-shutoff — the gap is mass produced via the homeostatic slacks without a corresponding import.

To truly enforce mass balance, the slack pseudofluxes need tight upper bounds — e.g. quadFractionFromUnity[m] ≤ 0 so the LP can only undershoot targets (physically meaningful: less biomass) and cannot overshoot by conjuring metabolites. That's a patch to modular_fba (or a monkeypatch in metabolism.py after the FBA model is built).

Why does growth continue after [GLC]ₑₓₜ hits zero? Three reasons stacked:
  1. ~17 molecules have unconstrained exchange bounds (AMMONIUM, OXYGEN-MOLECULE, Pi, SULFATE, WATER, K⁺, MG²⁺, NA⁺, Cl⁻, Fe²⁺/³⁺, Mn, Zn, Ca, Co, Ni, selenocysteine, CO₂). Once MM caps glucose uptake at 0, the LP keeps pulling on the others.
  2. The homeostatic objective pins internal metabolite concentrations toward fixed targets (set by getBiomassAsConcentrations). As the cell grows, these targets rescale — the LP keeps producing internal metabolites even when their upstream carbon supply is off.
  3. No carbon-balance constraint on biomass. The biomass reaction's coefficients assume carbon comes from somewhere; with GLC blocked the LP finds carbon in the CO₂ fixation / reverse-TCA routes that have catalysts present, at reduced efficiency but non-zero flux.
A proper exp→stat transition needs at least one of: a carbon-balanced biomass objective (no carbon source → no biomass), a ppGpp-coupled biomass downscaling, or hard flux bounds on the "promiscuous" inputs above when their carbon-equivalent contribution would violate a mass balance.

Calibration targets — Caglar 2017 doubling times

Per-replicate exponential doubling times reported in MOESM56. The simulation should hit these within the paper's 95% CI once nutrient-specific parameterization (carbon source, Na⁺, Mg²⁺) is in place. Currently the model is tuned only for the glucose base condition.

What this report does not cover yet

  • Non-glucose carbon sources: glycerol, gluconate, lactate. Requires media recipes + per-carbon v_max/K_m.
  • Ion stress: Na⁺, Mg²⁺ gradients from Caglar's figure 2B/C.
  • ppGpp-driven transition: mechanistic stationary- phase entry via the existing ppgpp_initiation pathway.
Generated by nutrient_growth_report.py · branch nutrient-growth. Calibration data: Caglar MU et al. (2017) Sci Rep 7, 45303.
